en.extract:
The black-eared wood quail (Odontophorus melanotis) is a bird species in the order Galliformes. Until recently, the species was thought to be part of the family Phasianidae (Old World quail) however DNA-DNA hybridization results determined that black-eared wood quail are only distantly related to Old World quail. As a result, black-eared wood quail have been placed in the family Odontophoridae (New World quail) and more specifically, in the category of wood quail (genus Odontophorus).
There is limited information available on this species, but black-eared wood quail are considered to be forest-adapted, monogamous, relatively large pheasant-like birds that can be found in tropical and subtropical forests of Central America. They feed on insects and fruit and can be solo or in small groups.


Description
The black-eared wood quail (Odontophorus melanotis) is a gallinaceous bird found in Central America. Its body is dark brown or black with a rufous breast. The most characteristic features of this bird are its unspotted chestnut crown and crest and its black face and throat. It has a black bill, blue-black legs and a purple bare ring around its eye. Females are close in appearance to males, but have a blue-black eye ring instead of purple, darker sides of the head and duller chestnut colors. 
The black-eared wood quail is very similar in appearance to the Rufous-fronted wood quail (Odontophorus erythrops), but the Rufous-fronted wood quail has a distinctive white jugular band that is absent in the black-eared wood quail.


Taxonomy
Black-eared wood quails are part of the taxonomic group Odontophorus. In the family Odontophoridae and subfamily Odontophorinae, Odontophorus is the largest genus with the greatest number of species and the largest collective geographic range. The species comprising this genus are typically large, forest dwelling birds. They are poorly observed, understudied and the least known group of all American gallinaceous birds.
Black-eared wood quails have often been considered to be a race of Rufous-fronted wood quail but there is no apparent evidence of intergradation. The current and most accurate nomenclature for the black-eared wood quail is Odontophorus melanotis and for the Rufous-fronted wood quail; Odontophorus erythrops.
There are two known subspecies of black-eared wood quail;

Odontophorus melanotis verecundus (Peters, 1929) . This subspecies' range encompasses the Caribbean slope of Honduras.
Odontophorus melanotis melanotis (Salvin, 1865). This subspecies has a distribution that includes South-East Honduras, Nicaragua as well as the Caribbean coast of Costa Rica and Panama.


Habitat and distribution
Despite the insufficient amount of data available, most species of Odontophorus, including the black-eared wood quail, are considered to be forest-adapted and typically found in tropical and lower subtropical forest habitats. They are territorial, ground dwelling and often found on the forest floor of virgin forests or in the vegetation of thick second growth.
The black-eared wood quail persists across the Caribbean slope of central America; including Honduras, Nicaragua and Costa Rica, as well as eastern Panama and likely the northwest of Columbia.


Behavior
The black-eared wood quail is presumed to be sedentary and is not known to migrate. Individuals of this species can be found alone, in pairs or in small groups of up to 10 or 12 individuals called coveys.


Vocalizations
Duetting is considered to be widespread within New World wood quail. Black-eared wood quail make soft, cooing or peeping conversational sounds among members of their covey. They also have an advertising call, which is a repetitive, ringing duet that can be sung by a lone bird and carries across long distances. The sound is described as "kooLAWlik kooLAWlik kooLAWK kooLAWK" or "LAWcooKLAWcoo". 


Diet
Very little information is available on the specific diet and feeding strategies of black-eared wood quail. However, black-eared wood quail, like other New World wood quail, are surmised to scratch in leaf-litter for insects and fallen fruit. 


Reproduction
The breeding season for black-eared wood quail is suspected to begin during the dry season of Panama (December to mid-April) and the birds are most likely monogamous. Nest have been found between trees, lined with leaves and grasses. Eggs have been found in clutches of 4 and were cream or white colored with brown spots. No additional information is available on the breeding and reproductive strategies of this species.


Conservation status and Threats
There is insufficient information available for many species of wood quail which has resulted in inaccurate conservation assessments based on unreliable data. Deforestation is the major threat to the species as well as hunting. The black-eared wood quail's population is decreasing and the total population is thought to be less than 50,000 birds. Nonetheless, black-eared wood quail are not considered to be globally threatened and are near threatened on the IUCN Red List, although more extensive studies and surveys are highly recommended and required for more accurate conservation conclusions.


References


External links
Ebird Black-Eared Wood-Quail
Birds of The World
BirdLife International

The '''black-eared wood quail''' ('''''Odontophorus melanotis''''') is a [[bird]] species in the order [[Galliformes]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Eitniear|first=Jack|title=Conservation of Quail in the Neotropics|publisher=Center for the Study of Tropical Birds, INC|year=1999|isbn=0-615-11802-X|pages=9, 26, 77, 78}}</ref> Until recently, the species was thought to be part of the family [[Phasianidae]] (Old World quail) however DNA-[[Dna hybridization|DNA hybridization]] results determined that black-eared wood quail are only distantly related to Old World quail.<ref name=":0" /> As a result, black-eared wood quail have been placed in the family [[New World quail|Odontophoridae]] (New World quail)<ref name=":0" /> and more specifically, in the category of wood quail (genus ''[[Wood quail|Odontophorus]]'').<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last=Johnsgard|first=Paul|author-link=Paul Johnsgard|date=1979|title=The American Wood Quails Odontophorus|url=https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/johnsgard/19|journal=World Pheasant Association Journal |volume=4 |pages=93–99}}</ref>

There is limited information available on this species, but black-eared wood quail are considered to be forest-adapted,<ref name=":0" /> monogamous,<ref name=":3" /> relatively large pheasant-like birds that can be found in tropical and subtropical forests of Central America.<ref name=":3" /> They feed on insects and fruit<ref name=":3" /> and can be solo or in small groups.<ref name=":5" />

== Description ==
The black-eared wood quail (''Odontophorus melanotis'') is a [[gallinaceous]] bird found in Central America. Its body is dark brown or black with a [[rufous]] breast.<ref name=":2">{{cite book |last1=Carroll |first1=John |chapter=Family Odontophoridae (New World Quails) |chapter-url=https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/natrespapers/648/ |editor1-last=del Hoyo |editor1-first=Josep |editor2-last=Elliott |editor2-first=Andrew |editor3-last=Sargatal |editor3-first=Jordi |editor4-last=Cabot |editor4-first=José |title=Handbook of the Birds of the World: New world vultures to guineafowl |date=1992 |publisher=Lynx Edicions |isbn=978-84-87334-15-3 }}</ref> The most characteristic features of this bird are its unspotted chestnut [[Crown (anatomy)|crown]] and [[Crest (feathers)|crest]] and its black face and throat.<ref name=":3">{{cite book |doi=10.2173/bow.bewqua1.01 |chapter=Black-eared Wood-Quail (Odontophorus melanotis) |title=Birds of the World |year=2020 |last1=Carroll |first1=John P. |last2=Kirwan |first2=Guy M. |s2cid=226408511 |editor-first1=Josep |editor-first2=Andrew |editor-first3=Jordi |editor-first4=David |editor-first5=Eduardo |editor-last1=Del Hoyo |editor-last2=Elliott |editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-last4=Christie |editor-last5=De Juana }}</ref> It has a black bill, blue-black legs and a purple bare ring around its eye.<ref name=":2" /> Females are close in appearance to males, but have a blue-black eye ring instead of purple, darker sides of the head and duller chestnut colors.<ref name=":2" /> 

The black-eared wood quail is very similar in appearance to the [[Rufous-fronted wood quail|Rufous-fronted wood quail (''Odontophorus erythrops'')]],<ref name=":3" /> but the Rufous-fronted wood quail has a distinctive white jugular band that is absent in the black-eared wood quail.<ref name=":2" />

== Taxonomy ==
Black-eared wood quails are part of the taxonomic group ''Odontophorus''.<ref name=":3" /> In the family Odontophoridae and subfamily Odontophorinae, ''Odontophorus'' is the largest genus with the greatest number of species and the largest collective geographic range.<ref name=":1" /> The species comprising this genus are typically large, forest dwelling birds.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> They are poorly observed, understudied and the least known group of all American gallinaceous birds.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" />

Black-eared wood quails have often been considered to be a race of Rufous-fronted wood quail<ref name=":2" /> but there is no apparent evidence of [[intergradation]].<ref name=":4">{{cite book |doi=10.2173/bow.rfwqua1.01 |chapter=Rufous-fronted Wood-Quail (Odontophorus erythrops) |title=Birds of the World |year=2020 |last1=Carroll |first1=John P. |last2=Kirwan |first2=Guy M. |last3=Boesman |first3=Peter F. D. |s2cid=240935109 |editor-first1=Josep |editor-first2=Andrew |editor-first3=Jordi |editor-first4=David |editor-first5=Eduardo |editor-last1=Del Hoyo |editor-last2=Elliott |editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-last4=Christie |editor-last5=De Juana }}</ref> The current and most accurate [[nomenclature]] for the black-eared wood quail is ''Odontophorus melanotis'' and for the Rufous-fronted wood quail; ''Odontophorus erythrops''.<ref name=":4" /><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Monroe|first1=Burt L.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3M7f_Y2zWDwC&q=odontophorus+melanotis&pg=PR15|title=A World Checklist of Birds|last2=Sibley|first2=Charles G.|date=1997-02-27|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-07083-5|language=en}}</ref>

There are two known subspecies of black-eared wood quail;

* ''Odontophorus melanotis verecundus'' (Peters, 1929) . This subspecies' range encompasses the Caribbean slope of Honduras.<ref name=":3" />
* ''Odontophorus melanotis melanotis'' (Salvin, 1865). This subspecies has a distribution that includes South-East Honduras, Nicaragua as well as the Caribbean coast of Costa Rica and Panama.<ref name=":3" />

== Habitat and distribution ==
Despite the insufficient amount of data available, most species of ''Odontophorus'', including the black-eared wood quail, are considered to be forest-adapted<ref name=":0" /> and typically found in tropical and lower subtropical forest habitats.<ref name=":3" /> They are territorial,<ref name=":0" /> ground dwelling and often found on the forest floor of [[Old-growth forest|virgin forests]] or in the vegetation of thick [[Secondary forest|second growth]].<ref name=":3" />

The black-eared wood quail persists across the Caribbean slope of central America; including Honduras, Nicaragua and Costa Rica,<ref name=":2" /> as well as eastern Panama and likely the northwest of Columbia.<ref name=":3" />

== Behavior ==
The black-eared wood quail is presumed to be sedentary<ref name=":3" /> and is not known to migrate. Individuals of this species can be found alone, in pairs or in small groups of up to 10 or 12 individuals<ref name=":2" /> called [[Herd|coveys]].<ref name=":5">{{cite thesis |last1=Hale |first1=Amanda Marie |year=2004 |title=Behavioral ecology and conservation of a neotropical wood-quail, ''Odontophorus leucolaemus'' |id={{ProQuest|305177586}} |oclc=61106014 }}</ref>

=== Vocalizations ===
Duetting is considered to be widespread within New World wood quail.<ref name=":5" /> Black-eared wood quail make soft, cooing or peeping conversational sounds among members of their covey.<ref name=":3" /> They also have an advertising call, which is a repetitive, ringing duet that can be sung by a lone bird and carries across long distances.<ref name=":3" /> The sound is described as "kooLAWlik kooLAWlik kooLAWK kooLAWK" or "LAWcooKLAWcoo".<ref name=":3" /> 

=== Diet ===
Very little information is available on the specific diet and feeding strategies of black-eared wood quail. However, black-eared wood quail, like other New World wood quail, are surmised to scratch in [[Plant litter|leaf-litter]] for insects and fallen fruit.<ref name=":3" /> 

=== Reproduction ===
The breeding season for black-eared wood quail is suspected to begin during the dry season of Panama (December to mid-April) and the birds are most likely [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]].<ref name=":3" /> Nest have been found between trees, lined with leaves and grasses.<ref name=":3" /> Eggs have been found in clutches of 4 and were cream or white colored with brown spots.<ref name=":2" /> No additional information is available on the breeding and reproductive strategies of this species.

== Conservation status and Threats ==
There is insufficient information available for many species of wood quail which has resulted in inaccurate conservation assessments based on unreliable data.<ref name=":0" /> [[Deforestation]] is the major threat to the species as well as hunting.<ref name=":3" /> The black-eared wood quail's population is decreasing<ref name=IUCN/> and the total population is thought to be less than 50,000 birds.<ref name=":3" /> Nonetheless, black-eared wood quail are not considered to be globally threatened and are near threatened on the IUCN Red List,<ref name=IUCN/><ref name=":3" /> although more extensive studies and surveys are highly recommended and required for more accurate conservation conclusions.<ref name=":0" />
